title: Psychrometric Calculations for Water Damage Restorers — GPP, EMC, and Dew Point Explained
description: Learn the psychrometric calculations that drive structural drying decisions — grains per pound (GPP), equilibrium moisture content (EMC), dew point, and specific humidity. Practical Australian restoration context with IICRC S500 applications.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Why Psychrometrics Matter in Restoration

Psychrometrics is the study of air-water vapour mixtures. In structural drying, understanding the psychrometric relationships between temperature, relative humidity, and moisture content in air allows you to accurately assess drying conditions, optimise equipment performance, and explain drying progress — or lack of it — to insurers and clients.

IICRC S500:2025 references psychrometric principles throughout, particularly in relation to equilibrium moisture content (EMC) and drying goal assessment.

## Grains Per Pound (GPP)

Grains per pound (GPP) is a measure of the absolute moisture content of air — the actual mass of water vapour held in one pound of dry air. Unlike relative humidity, GPP does not change when air temperature changes. This makes it the most reliable single indicator of the moisture load in a drying environment.

At a typical Australian summer indoor condition of 24°C and 60% relative humidity, the GPP is approximately 105–110 grains per pound. The target drying condition is to get the GPP inside the structure lower than the GPP outside, so that the dehumidification system is not fighting outdoor humidity when windows or doors are opened inadvertently.

A common benchmark: when the GPP inside the structure drops below 65–70 GPP, conditions are favourable for rapid evaporation from structural materials. This corresponds roughly to 50% RH at 21°C.

## Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)

EMC is the moisture content a material reaches when it has equilibrated with the surrounding air. Every material has an EMC curve — a relationship between the surrounding air's relative humidity (and temperature) and the moisture content the material will reach if left in that environment long enough.

For timber, the EMC at 65% RH and 21°C is approximately 12%. If you maintain the drying environment at 40% RH and 24°C, the EMC for the same timber drops to approximately 8%. This means the material will drive toward 8% — the structure is drying.

This is why temperature management is critical: warmer air with the same water vapour content has lower relative humidity, which in turn lowers the EMC target for structural materials and accelerates drying.

## Dew Point

The dew point is the temperature at which air becomes saturated — where condensation begins to form on cool surfaces. In restoration, dew point is important for assessing the risk of secondary condensation damage and for verifying that dehumidification is effective.

If the dew point inside a structure is higher than the surface temperature of any structural component, condensation will form on that surface — adding moisture rather than removing it. This is a common problem in poorly ventilated wall cavities during drying.

Monitoring dew point alongside temperature and relative humidity gives a complete picture of the drying environment and allows the restorer to detect conditions where secondary damage risk exists.

## Specific Humidity and the Dehumidifier Performance Test

A simple field test for dehumidifier performance uses specific humidity (the mass of water per kilogram of dry air, similar to GPP but in SI units). Measure the inlet and outlet specific humidity (or GPP) and temperature of the dehumidifier. If the difference between inlet and outlet is less than expected for the unit's rated capacity at the measured conditions, the unit may need maintenance or replacement.

Reference the manufacturer's performance chart for the specific temperature and inlet conditions being measured. Most dehumidifiers are rated at AHAM conditions (26.7°C, 60% RH), and performance drops significantly at lower temperatures or humidity levels.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're getting into psychrometrics — the science behind the air and moisture conditions in a drying environment. I know some people's eyes glaze over at the word 'psychrometrics', but stick with me because this directly affects your drying results and your ability to explain what's happening to an insurance adjuster.

Let's start with grains per pound, or GPP. This is the absolute moisture content of air — the actual mass of water vapour in a pound of dry air. The key thing about GPP is that it doesn't change when you heat the air. Relative humidity changes with temperature, GPP doesn't. That makes it your most reliable indicator of the actual moisture load in the space.

In practical terms: typical summer indoor conditions in Australia might run around 105 to 110 GPP. Your target is to get the indoor GPP below outdoor GPP so you're not importing humidity every time a door opens. When you get indoor GPP down to around 65 to 70 GPP — roughly 50% relative humidity at 21 degrees — you've created conditions for fast evaporation from structural materials.

Next up: equilibrium moisture content, or EMC. This is the moisture content a material will reach when it's sitting in a given air environment long enough. Timber at 65% RH and 21 degrees will settle at about 12% moisture content. Drop the RH to 40% and you drop the EMC to around 8%. That's the direction you're pushing the material toward — the lower the RH, the lower the EMC target, the faster the material dries.

This is why heating the space matters. Warmer air holds more moisture before it's saturated, so the same amount of water vapour in warm air gives you lower relative humidity and a lower EMC target. Temperature and dehumidification work together.

Dew point is the third piece. If any surface in the structure is cooler than the dew point of the air in the room, condensation will form on that surface. You're adding moisture instead of removing it. This is particularly sneaky in wall cavities — the outer face of external wall framing can be cool enough to condense moisture even while the room air feels dry. Always check wall cavity conditions on Class 3 jobs.

Finally, a quick field test for your dehumidifier: measure the GPP at the inlet and outlet. If the difference doesn't match the manufacturer's performance chart for the current conditions, the unit needs attention. Most dehumidifiers are rated at 26.7 degrees and 60% RH — performance drops noticeably below those conditions.

RestoreAssist tracks temperature, relative humidity, and GPP readings through your inspection monitoring to show drying progression trends. Head to restoreassist.com.au to learn more.
